components.name: Starship Ship
components.role: The orbital spacecraft stage of the Starship system. Carries payloads to orbit and beyond Earth's atmosphere.
components.whyItMatters: Starship Ship is the world's most powerful upper stage ever built. It's fully reusable, refuelable, and designed to carry humans and cargo to the Moon, Mars, and beyond.

components.name: Nosecone / Payload Fairing
components.role: The forward section of Starship that houses payloads and provides aerodynamic entry for return through atmosphere.
components.whyItMatters: The nosecone doubles as the payload bay door. It's designed to open in orbit to deploy satellites and close again for return. No payload fairing jettisoned = fully reusable.

components.name: Thermal Protection System
components.role: Hexagonal heat shield tiles protecting the ship from reentry temperatures up to 1,500°C.
components.whyItMatters: Starship must survive reentry from orbital velocity (~7.8 km/s). The TPS is the difference between survival and burning up. First time a vehicle this large uses thermal tiles for reentry.
components.status: In development - tile adhesion improvements ongoing

components.name: Forward Aerodynamic Flaps (Grippers)
components.role: Four hypersonic flap surfaces for aerodynamic control during reentry and descent.
components.whyItMatters: These flaps are critical for controlled aerodynamic descent. The forward flaps also serve as 'gripper' interfaces for the Mechazilla tower catch mechanism.

components.name: Ship Engine Section
components.role: Houses the vacuum-optimized Raptor engines and associated propellant feed systems.
components.whyItMatters: Vacuum engines have much larger nozzles optimized for performance above ~10km altitude. Ship uses these exclusively since it fires in vacuum only during nominal flight.

components.name: Raptor Engine (Vacuum)
components.role: Optimized for thrust in vacuum. Fixed geometry, non-throttling.
components.whyItMatters: The Raptor is a full-flow staged combustion engine - the most efficient and sophisticated rocket engine ever built. The vacuum variant maximizes specific impulse for orbital operations.

components.name: Super Heavy Booster
components.role: The first stage booster. Provides most of the ascent thrust to get Starship to space.
components.whyItMatters: The largest and most powerful rocket stage ever built. 33 Raptor engines produce more thrust than the Saturn V. It's designed to be caught by the launch tower for rapid reuse.

components.name: Grid Fins
components.role: Aerobic control surfaces for booster during descent. Provide roll control and steering.
components.whyItMatters: Grid fins provide aerodynamic stability during boostback burns and descent. They fold flush against the booster during ascent to minimize drag.

components.name: Booster Engine Section
components.role: The bottom section housing 33 sea-level Raptor engines arranged in a complex pattern.
components.whyItMatters: 33 engines in a precise pattern controlled by the flight computer. Can tolerate multiple engine failures and still complete the mission. This is the most powerful rocket stage ever.

components.name: Raptor Engine (Sea Level)
components.role: Optimized for operation at sea level. Gimballing for thrust vector control.
components.whyItMatters: Sea level Raptors must operate in the thick atmosphere and provide thrust vector control. The throttling capability allows thrust modulation for max-Q and landing.

components.name: Hot Staging Ring
components.role: Enables 'hot separation' where Ship engines ignite before booster separation for cleaner stage separation.
components.whyItMatters: Traditional staging separates then ignites upper stage engines. Hot staging ignites the Ship while still attached, using booster's own tail-chute effect for separation. This saves mass and complexity.

components.name: Interstage Adapter
components.role: Structural connection between Ship and Booster. Houses hot staging components.
components.whyItMatters: The interstage must survive launch loads and transmit forces between stages. The clamp band design allows for quick connect/disconnect for turnaround.

components.name: Orbital Launch Mount / Mechazilla
components.role: The launch infrastructure including the 'chopstick' catch arms for booster and ship recovery.
components.whyItMatters: No legs. No landing pads. Mechazilla catches the booster and ship mid-air and returns them to the launch mount for rapid reuse. This eliminates landing gear and massive ground infrastructure.

components.name: Mechazilla Catch System
components.role: The giant arms ('chopsticks') that catch and handle both Super Heavy and Starship.
components.whyItMatters: First time in history a launch tower catches the booster and ship. This enables faster turnaround than any existing landing system and eliminates all ground touchdown systems.
